chunk_summary: Guidelines for inpatient discharge readiness and step-down handover in severe acute malnutrition (SAM) management, emphasizing clinical stability, feeding criteria, and follow-up protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand clinical stability thresholds for inpatient discharge readiness in SAM management.
Learn functional criteria for safe transition from inpatient to outpatient care.
Identify key follow-up protocols and danger signs post-discharge.
Recognize the importance of linking families to outpatient nutrition services.
key_facts: Discharge readiness requires no hypoglycemia or hypothermia for at least 24 hours.
Feeding readiness includes sustained intake of 120–130 kcal/kg/day with weight gain ≥5 g/kg/day for 3 consecutive days.
Therapeutic feeding prescription and danger signs must be documented at discharge.
First follow-up should be scheduled within 7 days post-discharge.
Early weight loss in oedematous SAM may reflect oedema resolution, not deterioration.
reference_quote: Discharge readiness is a clinical stability threshold, distinct from outpatient program 'cure' criteria requiring anthropometric recovery and oedema-free duration. (Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, 2011)
document_key_topics: Inpatient discharge readiness criteria for SAM
Linking families to outpatient nutrition services
